summary: High‑energy set with killer Crazy Fingers, Day Job & tight jams
blurb: The show opens with a scorching Keep Your Day Job that launches into a soaring Playing In The Band > Crazy Fingers sequence. Highlights include an incendiary Crazy Fingers jam, punchy Deal, and a seamless Good Lovin' finish. Jerry’s guitar is on fire while Brent adds adventurous keys throughout.
review: Richmond Coliseum on October 8, 1983 delivered one of the most electrifying shows of the year. The first set kicks off with a fiery Keep Your Day Job that immediately sets a high‑octane tone, leading into a flawless Playing In The Band > Crazy Fingers medley where Jerry’s “sick” guitar tone and Brent’s slashing chords weave together in perfect sync. Throughout the night the band never lets up—Crazy Fingers rolls into a tight Truckin', Spoonful slides seamlessly into Wharf Rat, and a surprise Sugar Magnolia tease fuels a joyous Good Lovin' climax. The rhythm section is rock solid: Phil’s heavy “Phil‑Zone” bass underpins every groove, while Mickey and Bill lock in with muscular drumming and melodic flourishes. Bob Weir adds aggressive counterpoint and standout vocals on Good Lovin'. In the encore, U.S. Blues caps a night that feels both tight and wildly exploratory—truly a high‑energy Richmond classic.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All recordings (except one mixed) are overwhelmingly positive, repeatedly praising the high‑energy performance, standout jams (Crazy Fingers, Playing In The Band), and strong band cohesion. No consistent negative comments about musical execution were found.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear audience tape with front‑of‑stage feel, excellent balance and detail that captures the high‑energy performance without muddiness.

key_highlights: Crazy Fingers > Playing In The Band sequence rolls into a tight Truckin' jam.
Keep Your Day Job opener drives the show’s relentless momentum.
Brent’s adventurous keyboard chords add color, especially during Crazy Fingers.
Phil’s heavy, clean bass (the classic Phil‑Zone sound) grounds every groove.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivered an incendiary performance—emotive, powerful vocals and a guitar tone described as “sick” and “nice long time finishing” on Crazy Fingers, staying ‘on fire’ throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Phil’s bass was heavy yet clean, delivering the classic Phil‑Zone sound that anchored every jam and added rumbling fills during the high‑energy sections.
band_performance.Bob: Weir provided aggressive rhythm guitar counterpoint, strong vocals on Good Lovin', and a notable whammy‑bar flourish during Crazy Fingers, keeping the band’s push forward.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s adventurous chords and slashing playing added color throughout, especially shining in the Crazy Fingers jam and the mid‑song keyboard textures.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s cowbell intro and tight, muscular drumming helped drive the second‑set momentum, with melodic touches that lifted the jams.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann laid down a solid groove, delivering powerful post‑drum sections praised for their energy and cohesion.
